data.title: CBS denies it pulled Colbert's Talarico interview
data.description:
CBS is denying an accusation from Stephen Colbert, the network’s top comic and host of The Late Show, that it prevented him from airing an interview with Texas Democratic Senate candidate James Talarico.

Colbert during his Monday show told viewers that attorneys for CBS told him “in no uncertain terms” that “we could not have him on the broadcast,” referring to Talarico, a member of the Texas House.

“Not only could I not have him on, I could not mention me not having him on,” the host continued. “And because my network clearly does not want us to talk about this, let’s talk about this.”

CBS in a statement to The Hill denied Colbert’s account.

“The Late Show was not prohibited by CBS from broadcasting the interview with Rep. James Talarico,” a spokesperson for the network said. “The show was provided legal guidance that the broadcast could trigger the FCC equal-time rule for two other candidates, including Rep. Jasmine Crockett, and presented options for how the equal time for other candidates could be fulfilled.”

It was the Late Show itself, CBS said, which “decided to present the interview through its YouTube channel with on-air promotion on the broadcast rather than potentially providing the equal-time options.”

The Hill reached out to the Late Show for comment.

The back-and-forth comes as President Trump’s FCC chair, Brendan Carr, has floated doing away with a department rule that exempts daytime and late-night talk shows from the FCC’s equal-time protection, which dictates all candidates for public office receive equal time on public airwaves.

Colbert, during his monologue, tore into Carr, mocking his appearance and arguing he is motivated by a desire to please Trump.

“Let’s just call this what it is: Donald Trump’s administration wants to silence anyone who says anything bad about Trump on TV, because all Trump does is watch TV,” the host said.

FCC Commissioner Ana Gomez, a Democrat, also blasted Paramount, CBS’ parent company, over the dispute, saying it amounted to political censorship.

Paramount has made headlines with sweeping changes to its news division in recent months and last year canceled Colbert’s show, which is slated to come off the air this spring.

The broadcaster at the time said Colbert’s cancelation was necessary given rising financial pressures facing the company.

Critics accused to company of seeking favor with Trump as it sought the administration’s approval for a merger with Skydance. And on Tuesday, Paramount renewed talks with Warner Bros. Discovery to acquire its entertainment and news assets.

data.title: Jesse Jackson Jr.: Father 'did not see himself as a politician'
data.description:
Former Rep. Jesse Jackson Jr. (D-Ill.), the son of civil rights leader Rev. Jesse Jackson, said Tuesday that his father “did not see himself as a politician” but rather viewed politics as a vehicle to help marginalized communities.

“He saw himself as a public servant. He believed in servant leadership. He saw himself as a seminarian. He saw politics as a way to carry out the ministry of feeding the hungry, of clothing the naked, of providing health care for those without health care, of providing food for people who could not eat, and resurrecting the spirits of people,” Jackson told CNN anchor Wolf Blitzer on “The Situation Room.”

“And so the broadest possible platform that was available to my father in the American context was public life,” he added.

The elder Jackson died early Tuesday after battling the neurodegenerative disorder progressive supranuclear palsy (PSP). He was 84.

Jackson worked alongside Dr. Martin Luther King Jr. and his son noted Tuesday that he “did the best he could to keep that flame alive” following King’s assassination. That included founding the Rainbow PUSH coalition, a social and economic justice organization that worked to implement more diversity in politics by registering new voters, among other initiatives.

Jackson Jr. said his father had been concerned toward the end of his life about recent court decisions that could “undermine” Section Two of the Voting Rights Act of 1965, which prohibits racial gerrymandering and other redistricting practices that dilute minority votes.

Jackson Jr. also noted that his father was “deeply troubled” by rising polarization in the U.S. and that he had recently reached out to President Trump to discuss “the unique challenge before the President of the United States to bring all Americans together.”

“One of his latest communications to the president was about tenor. It was about tone. It was about spirit, energy,” Jackson Jr. said, indicating that his father was hopeful his “unique relationship” with Trump might bring about a shift.

Jackson Jr. said his father “unfortunately” never got a call back.

“But it’s never too late. It’s never too late to do right, and my father sincerely believed that,” he quickly added.

Trump described Jackson in a Truth Social post as a “force of nature like few others before him.”

“He was a good man, with lots of personality, grit, and ‘street smarts.’ He was very gregarious – Someone who truly loved people!” Trump wrote.

data.title: Nancy Guthrie search: No DNA hit from glove found near house, says sheriff
data.description:
(NewsNation) — DNA from gloves found near Nancy Guthrie’s house did not get a hit in CODIS, the FBI’s database for DNA from criminal offenders.

The Pima County Sheriff’s Office confirmed Tuesday that the DNA was submitted to a lab in Florida and then uploaded to the system in search of a match.

The department said that additional DNA found at the residence is also being analyzed.

The search for Guthrie has now entered a third week, with investigators also working to identify a suspect based on surveillance videos and photos.

The 84-year-old mother of TV anchor Savannah Guthrie was reported missing on Feb. 1. Authorities believe she was abducted from her home in the late night or early morning hours before her family reported her missing.

On Monday, investigators publicly stated the family, including spouses of family members, had been cleared in the investigation. In Tuesday’s statement, the sheriff’s office said at this point in the investigation, no family members have been identified as suspects.

In addition to pursuing DNA evidence, authorities have been working to use videos from a camera at Nancy Guthrie’s front door to try to identify a suspect.

FBI agents visited gun stores in the area with a list of roughly 40 names and photographs, NewsNation’s Brian Entin reported, asking if any of those individuals had been in stores or bought a weapon.

The masked person is seen on the footage appearing to tamper with the camera, at one point holding up shrubbery to hide their face.

Authorities have described the suspect as being of average build, between 5 feet 9 inches and 5 feet 10 inches tall. One line of investigation has centered around tracking down clothing and items seen in the video, including a backpack and holster.

Investigators have also been using a device to try to detect any signal from Guthrie’s pacemaker, although that device has a very limited range.

Ransom notes were sent to the media in the early days of the case, with two ransom deadlines having passed. Investigators have not publicly confirmed whether the suspected kidnappers communicated with the family.

TMZ has also received four emails from someone offering information on the identity of the kidnappers in exchange for money. Following the latest email, the outlet offered to help get information from the emailer to the FBI and ensure the writer gets the reward if the information proves to be legitimate.

The FBI is offering a reward of up to $100,000 for information leading to Guthrie’s recovery or the arrest and conviction of anyone involved in her disappearance.

Anyone with “actionable information” is asked to contact 1-800-CALL-FBI or the Pima County Sheriff’s Department nonemergency line at 520-351-4900.

data.title: Randy Fine faces censure and resignation calls over post on Muslims and dogs
data.description:
A wave of Democrats are calling for Rep. Randy Fine (R-Fla.) to be formally reprimanded or to resign over a social media post he made saying that he would choose dogs over Muslims. 

Fine posted on X on Sunday: “If they force us to choose, the choice between dogs and Muslims is not a difficult one.”

House Minority Leader Hakeem Jeffries (D-N.Y.) in a statement on Tuesday called Fine “a disgrace to the United States Congress” and “an Islamaphobic, disgusting bigot.”

Jeffries called on Speaker Mike Jonson (R-La.) and House Republicans to respond, slamming them for continuing “to remain silent.”

“Republican leaders must hold this so-called Member of Congress accountable,” Jeffries said. “Political violence, xenophobia and hate crimes against the Muslim community are on the rise. House Democrats will not let the racist and bigoted behavior of Randy Fine go unchecked. Accountability is coming to all of these sick extremists when the gavels change hands in November, if not sooner.”

While Jeffries did not provide details on what actions Democrats were planning to take against Fine, other members were more specific.

“Fine must be censured. It’s about morality and decency, not politics,” Rep. Ro Khanna (D-Calif.) posted in response to Fine, calling the comment “disgusting bigotry.”

A censure is a formal reprimand by a vote of the House that often comes with consequences like being stripped of committee assignments. Any member can force a snap vote on a censure resolution over the objections of GOP leadership.

Rep. Alexandria Ocasio-Cortez (D-N.Y.) called Fine’s post “genuinely one of the most disgusting statements I have ever seen issued by an American official.”

“It should not stop shocking us that the Republican Party openly embraces this. Fine should be censured & stripped of committees. To ignore this is to accept and normalize it,” she added.

Rep. Robert Garcia (D-Calif.), ranking member on the House Oversight and Government Reform Committee, said Johnson should “should strip [Fine] of all committees and assignments and he should be forced to resign.”

The criticism went well beyond Democratic members of Congress. California Gov. Gavin Newsom (D) told Fine: “Resign now, you racist slob.”

After his initial post, Fine gave context to his comment by posting a screenshot of a post from pro-Palestinian activist Nerdeen Kiswani from last week in which she said: “Finally, NYC is coming to Islam. Dogs definitely have a place in society, just not as indoor pets. Like we’ve said all along, they are unclean.” Kiswani later told NBC News that the comment was satire about the conversation about dog waste in New York City after a recent snow storm.

In response to the criticism, a defiant Fine on Monday and Tuesday repeatedly posted a Gadsden flag — which normally features a snake and the words “Don’t tread on me” — with a puppy in place of a snake.

Responding to Jeffries specifically, Fine posted the Gadsden flag with a chihuahua in a sombrero — in reference to a post from President Trump last year that put Jeffries in a sombrero.

Fine is no stranger to sparking controversy or showing hostility toward Palestinian activists. Last year, when commenting on reported starvation in Gaza, Fine posted:  “Release the hostages. Until then, starve away. (This is all a lie anyway. It amazes me that the media continues to regurgitate Muslim terror propaganda.)”

It was not only Democrats, however, who expressed dismay with Fine’s most recent post about dogs.

Conservative radio and podcast host Megyn Kelly responded: “wtf is this,” while Rasmussen Reports pollster Mark Mitchell commented: “Hey @GOP Not a good look.”

Conservative provocateur Laura Loommer, on the other hand, praised Fine for taking a “patriotic stand against Muslims trying to BAN DOGS.”

Fine’s office did not immediately respond to a request for comment.

data.title: Whoopi Goldberg on Epstein files mention: 'I wasn't his girlfriend. I wasn't his friend'
data.description:
Whoopi Goldberg says she was neither Jeffrey Epstein’s friend nor girlfriend after being “dragged” for the appearance of her name in files related to the late convicted sex offender. 

“In the name of transparency,” Goldberg said while co-hosting “The View” on Tuesday, “my name is in the files.”

She read a 2013 note included among the files, which asked an intermediary for Epstein if the disgraced financier would want to lend his private plane for her use to travel to a charity event.

“They are saying do you want to offer your G-II?” Goldberg, referring to the email, told fellow hosts Sara Haines, Joy Behar, Sunny Hostin and guest co-host Savannah Chrisley. 

“This is my point, when I tell you people are trying to turn me into — I wasn’t his girlfriend. I wasn’t his friend,” Goldberg said. 

“You were too old for him,” one of the co-hosts replied.

“I was not only too old, but it was at a time where this is just not — you used to have to have facts before you said stuff,” Goldberg said.

“But Trump is on the list 38,000 times,” Behar said.

President Trump, who has not been accused of any wrongdoing in regard to the Epstein case, told reporters on Saturday that the latest release of documents absolved him.

“I can’t speak to him,” Goldberg said of the president, “but I’m speaking about me because I’m getting dragged. People actually believe that I was with [Epstein.]”

“It’s like, honey, come on,” she added.

“Every man that I’ve ever been with, you’ve known about him because either the [National] Enquirer wrote it about it. People wrote about this stuff,” Goldberg, 70, said.

“No, I didn’t get on the plane. Because you know what I would have to do to get on the plane?” Goldberg said, noting her longtime fear of flying.

data.title: Sharpton honors mentor Jesse Jackson as 'the steady voice that guided me'
data.description:
The Rev. Al Sharpton on Tuesday said his mentor and late civil right rights leader the Rev. Jesse L. Jackson called him into purpose. 

“He trusted me with responsibility and saw something in me before I fully saw it in myself. That is the measure of a true mentor: they do not just teach you; they name you,” Sharpton said in a statement.

Jackson, a protege of the Rev. Martin Luther King Jr. and a national icon during the civil rights movement during the 1960s, died on Tuesday at age 84. He was part of the “Greenville Eight,” a group of eight Black students who tried to desegregate a public library in protest of racial segregation policies in the South. 

Jackson was also founder of the Rainbow PUSH Coalition, an ordained minister, the inaugural shadow senator from the District of Columbia and a CNN show host.

In 1969, Jackson appointed the civil rights and social justice activist Sharpton as youth director of the Brooklyn branch of the organization Operation Breadbasket. The organization was an extension of the Southern Christian Leadership Conference, founded by King.

Sharpton said Jackson appointed him to the position when he was “still a teenager trying to find my place in this struggle.”

“One of the greatest honors of my life was learning at his side. He reminded me that faith without action is just noise. He taught me that protest must have purpose, that faith must have feet, and that justice is not seasonal, it is daily work,” Sharpton said.

Sharpton also said that Jackson became more than a public figure to him. He also became his children’s godfather.

“He taught them, and me, that trying is as important as triumph. That you do not wait for the dream to come true; you work to make it real,” Sharpton said. “Because of him, I learned that leadership is not about being seen, it is about seeing others. Because of him, I learned that our lives mean the most when they are spent widening the circle of who belongs.

“Today, I grieve not only a leader, but the steady voice that guided me when the road was uncertain,” Sharpton said. “I am heartbroken. But I am eternally grateful that God allowed me to walk beside a man who helped bend the arc of history and shaped the arc of my own life.”

data.title: Iran's supreme leader threatens to sink US warships as nuclear talks continue
data.description:
Iranian Supreme Leader Ayatollah Ali Khamenei threatened on Tuesday to sink U.S. warships amid continuing nuclear talks between the Middle Eastern nation and the U.S. 

“The Americans constantly say that they’ve sent a warship toward Iran,” Khamenei posted on the social platform X. “Of course, a warship is a dangerous piece of military hardware.”

“However, more dangerous than that warship is the weapon that can send that warship to the bottom of the sea,” he added.

This stark warning comes as officials from both the U.S. and Iran met for a second round of discussions about Iran’s nuclear program in Geneva on Tuesday. 

U.S. representatives are pushing for a new deal with the country after the first Trump administration withdrew from the Joint Comprehensive Plan of Action, an Obama-era nuclear deal that placed limits on Iran’s enrichment of uranium in exchange for sanctions relief. 

President Trump expressed his hope for the negotiations to reporters on Friday, warning that if the two countries fail to reach an agreement it would be a “bad day for Iran, very bad.” 

The president also confirmed that the U.S. is deploying the world’s largest aircraft carrier, the U.S.S. Gerald R. Ford, to the Middle East. The vessel and its escort ships will join American warships currently positioned in the U.S. Central Command (CENTCOM) area. 

Trump has warned he plans to issue commands to use military force against Iran if a nuclear deal is not reached and if the Iranian regime continues its deadly crackdowns on protesters. An estimated 6,159 people have been reported dead, according to activists’ reports in January, after demonstrations broke out in the country at the end of last year over the plummeting value of the country’s currency. 

Iran has been conducting maritime military training sessions this week with live missiles in the Strait of Hormuz, a major thoroughfare for international trade. CENTCOM had previously cautioned the country against any “unsafe and unprofessional behavior” at the end of January. 

In another X post on Tuesday, the Iranian leader pushed back against Trump’s claims of U.S. military supremacy. 

“The US President keeps saying that they have the strongest military force in the world,” Khamenei said. “The strongest military force in the world may at times be struck so hard that it cannot get up again.”

data.title: 60 percent say they're confident midterms will be counted fairly: Polling
data.description:
Trust in the accuracy and integrity of elections is declining among U.S. voters amid concerns over mid-cycle redistricting attempts and the potential that Immigration and Customs Enforcement (ICE) officers could be present at the polls this November, according to new research from the UC San Diego Center for Transparent and Trusted Elections.

A survey of over 11,000 eligible voters found that trust in elections among Democrats, Republicans and independents has fallen sharply since the 2024 presidential election, with only 60 percent of Americans now saying they are confident that votes in the 2026 midterms will be counted accurately.

Independents had the lowest rate of trust at 57 percent, followed by Democrats and Republicans at 64 percent and 65 percent, respectively.  

Researchers noted that the overall 60 percent figure represented a 17-point decline from where trust stood following the most recent presidential election and that each political party experienced a double-digit drop.

“The large partisan divide over trust in elections that was present before President Trump’s victory closed just after it, and now trust among all political groups has been declining in parallel,” a report outlining the survey’s findings stated.  

Republicans’ concerns centered mostly on mail-in ballots and whether non-citizens would be prevented from voting, with about half expressing skepticism on both of those issues.  

That skepticism was shared more closely among independents at 35 percent and 29 percent, respectively, than Democrats. Twenty percent of Democrats voiced distrust that mail-in ballots would be counted accurately, and only 11 percent were skeptical that non-citizens would be kept from casting ballots, according to the survey.

President Trump has often claimed, without evidence, that mail-in ballots and instances of undocumented immigrants voting contributed to widespread fraud in the 2020 presidential election.

Those unsubstantiated allegations have formed the basis for Trump’s recent call to “nationalize” elections and Republican pressure for a national voter ID law that would require Americans to show photo ID when voting in federal elections and provide proof of U.S. citizenship to register.

That push prompted alarm bells from many Democrats, who suggested the Trump administration may try to “interfere” with the midterm elections, including by positioning immigration enforcement officers around polling locations.

“I am, we are all, greatly afraid with these roving ICE vans that we see in Minneapolis and other cities. Could those ICE roving vans be used to try to go and intimidate voters at the polling station?” Sen. Mark Warner (D-Va.) said earlier this month.

The UC San Diego survey found that about one-third of voters in all political groups thought it was likely that ICE officers would be present at voting locations in their area this November; however, the level of concern varied across racial and ethic groups.

Hispanic and Black voters indicated the greatest concern about facing possible intimidation at the polls at 46 percent each, compared to 10 percent of white voters, according to the report.

The survey also found shared concern among voters about the impact of partisan redistricting attempts, regardless of party affiliation.

Fifty-one percent of Democrats, 48 percent of Republicans and 34 percent of independents said they were skeptical that congressional district lines are drawn to fairly reflect what voters want.

“While members of each party primarily assign blame to the other party’s leaders for gerrymandering over the past year, nearly a quarter of each party also expressed dissatisfaction with their own party about this,” the report stated.

The survey was conducted among 11,406 eligible U.S. voters between Dec. 19 and Jan. 12. It has a margin of error of 0.9 percentage points.

data.title: Live updates: DHS fight drags on as Democrats make new offer; Tricia McLaughlin to step down
data.description:
Democrats have made a new offer to the White House in negotiations to reopen the Department of Homeland Security, which shut down on Saturday over a fight centered on Immigration and Customs Enforcement (ICE).

Democrats initially sent a 10-point plan featuring their wish list on Feb. 4, headlined by calls to tighten warrant requirements, unmask agents engaging in field operations and end roving patrols. That was sent to Republicans in the form of legislative text on Feb. 7. 

The White House responded in kind with a proposal of its own days later. They hoped that would convince Democrats to support a stopgap measure to keep DHS open until a final deal was struck. That did not happen, with lawmakers leaving on Thursday for a pre-planned recess around Presidents’ Day. 

The odds of a deal in the coming days look slim, though the fact that few details have emerged over the back-and-forth offers also suggests the talks are serious.

Meanwhile, DHS spokesperson Tricia McLaughlin, a staunch defender of President Trump’s immigration agenda, is expected to depart the agency as soon as next week. 

On Tuesday, special envoy Steve Witkoff and Trump’s son-in-law Jared Kushner met with Iranian officials in Geneva to continue talks about the nation’s nuclear facilities. It marked the second meeting following a six-hour round of talks in Oman earlier this month and comes a day after Iranian naval forces began conducting military exercises in the Strait of Hormuz.

Witkoff and Kushner are also expected to participate in a separate set of talks, between Ukraine and Russia, later Tuesday afternoon in Geneva. 

The president is back at the White House following a President’s Day weekend spent at his Mar-a-Lago resort in Palm Beach, Fla. The House and Senate are out this week.

Follow along live below.

data.title: Hakeem Jeffries resists pressure to say 'Abolish ICE' — good!
data.description:
People are furious with House Minority Leader Hakeem Jeffries right now.   

On “The Joy Reid Show,” he refused to say the words “abolish ICE” — even as frustration with the agency is at a boiling point.  

Podcast host Wajahat Ali came armed with facts, citing CATO Institute polling showing 65 percent of people taken by ICE had no convictions, and 93 percent had no violent convictions. So he wanted to know why taxpayer dollars were being used to support this. 

Here’s the exchange:  

Ali: “What you’re telling us is that you want our taxpayer dollars to pay for a lawless, masked, armed agency to continue terrorizing our cities. I’m trying to figure out how you as a leader can be telling Americans that their taxpayer dollars should be going to ICE.” 

Jeffries: “I don’t understand anything that you just said when I’ve made clear that taxpayer dollars should be used to make life more affordable for the American people, not brutalize or kill them — that’s the whole reason we’re in this fight.”  

Ali pressed again: “So you agree with me and Joy — Abolish ICE?” 

Jeffries: “Listen, I’m gonna use the language that I want to use and you can use the language that you want to use.” 

That answer lit up social media. But here’s the nuance: ICE’s brutal tactics are unpopular. So is border insecurity. Voters can oppose lawless enforcement and still want order at the border. Both things are true.  

Right now, Democrats are holding up Homeland Security funding to demand oversight reforms. Funding has expired, triggering a partial shutdown. Some parts of DHS are squeezed; ICE and CBP are not. They’re flush with funding from last year’s “One Big Beautiful Bill.”  

So there’s a bind — Democrats want to curb ICE’s excesses without sounding like they’re walking away from enforcement altogether.  

The killings of Renee Nicole Good and Alex Pretti in Minneapolis were horrific. It was all foreseeable. It reflects an enforcement approach that rewards escalation over restraint. And yes, moments like that make people demand dramatic action.  

But slogans and strategy are not the same thing.  

Slogans like “Defund the police” or “Abolish ICE” don’t connect with the voters Democrats need in purple and red districts. It almost seems like Democrats are walking into a trap set by the president and his advisers, giving Republicans the opportunity to label them as extreme. While ICE’s responsibilities could be reassigned, with Democrats in the minority in Congress, the focus has to be on what’s realistically achievable right now.  

That’s the political reality.  

There is a serious argument here: Abolish abuses. End masked squads. End unaccountable force. End opaque detention systems. Tie funding to transparency and professional standards. Make enforcement lawful, targeted and humane.  

That is not softness. That is governance.  

Americans want accountability without cruelty. Enforcement without lawlessness. A system that works and respects rights.  

The question isn’t whether ICE should exist in theory. It’s whether it can operate within the bounds of law and public trust.  

And if Democrats want to win the power to change it, they have to make that case carefully, and convincingly, to the country they’re trying to lead.  

Lindsey Granger is a NewsNation contributor and co-host of The Hill’s commentary show “Rising.” This column is an edited transcription of her on-air commentary.
